nativeRange.habitat: Sandy reef slopes, lagoons, and areas around continental islands; typically rests free-living on sandy substrate rather than attaching to hard reef

decorPreferences: sand bed placement (free-living, not mounted on rock)
level sandy substrate away from rockwork edges

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented extending feeding tentacles at night and readily accepts target-fed meaty foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, and finely minced seafood. Spot feeding 1–2 times per week tends to support better coloration and tissue health. Circulation should be reduced or paused during feeding to keep food in contact with polyp.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals placed in direct contact
fast-sweeper corals (Hammer, Torch, Frogspawn) within 10 cm
sand-stirring fish that may bury or abrade the polyp
gall crabs (Lithoscaptus semperi — a known natural pest)

summary: Trachyphyllia geoffroyi is a monotypic free-living LPS coral found on sandy Indo-Pacific lagoon floors. Generally considered intermediate care; placed directly on a sand bed with low flow and moderate-low light (40–100 PAR).
careNotes:
Trachyphyllia geoffroyi is the sole species in its genus and is commonly traded as "Open Brain Coral" or "Wellsophyllia" (Wellsophyllia radiata is a junior synonym). Its colorful fleshy tissue — ranging from green and blue to red and brown — makes it a popular reef showpiece.

Placement is the most critical care factor. This coral is naturally free-living, resting on sandy lagoon substrate rather than attaching to rock. In the aquarium it should be placed directly on the sand bed in a level spot away from rockwork edges. Rough surfaces can abrade and damage its delicate tissue as it expands and contracts. Allow at least 5 cm of clearance from neighboring corals to account for sweeper tentacle reach and tissue expansion.

Lighting: Generally advised to keep at 40–100 PAR. Most sources converge on low-to-moderate intensity; the coral tends to bleach when exposed to PAR above ~150 for extended periods. New specimens are best acclimated by starting at the lower end of the range and adjusting over 2–4 weeks. Both the green and red color morphs thrive under the same lighting conditions.

Flow: Low flow is preferred. Gentle, indirect circulation is enough to keep the surface clean of detritus. Strong direct flow risks physical damage to the soft tissue and has been known to flip lightweight specimens.

Feeding: Though capable of surviving on photosynthesis alone, T. geoffroyi has been documented benefiting from weekly target feeding of meaty foods (mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, minced seafood). Feeding tentacles typically extend at night but will appear during the day when food is detected. Reduce pump flow during feeding sessions to keep food in contact with the polyp.

Nutrients: Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–10 ppm, phosphate 0.05–0.1 ppm) are generally considered acceptable; ultra-low-nutrient systems can contribute to poor coloration.

Aggression: Moderate — this coral extends short sweeper tentacles that can sting neighbors. The reach tends to increase as colonies grow.

Aquaculture status: T. geoffroyi is rarely aquacultured and is generally wild-collected. Indonesia exported over 60,000 specimens in 2005 alone (IUCN). Fragging is not practical given its single-polyp or limited-colony structure and slow skeletal growth rate. The species is listed as Near Threatened on the IUCN Red List.

Family note: Some older retail sources list the family as "Trachyphylliidae"; current molecular taxonomy (WoRMS, ITIS) places this species within Merulinidae.

Known natural pest: The gall crab Lithoscaptus semperi has been documented burrowing into Trachyphyllia skeletons. Inspect new specimens carefully and dip as a precaution.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Captive propagation is not practiced in the hobby — the species does not lend itself to fragging due to its single free-living polyp structure and slow skeletal growth. Aquaculture supply is essentially nonexistent; virtually all trade specimens are wild-collected.

additional.name: ReefChasers — Trachyphyllia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (77–84°F / 25–28.9°C), pH (8.0–8.4), dKH (8–9), Ca (400–450 ppm), lighting (100–150 PAR), flow, placement, and care level (listed as ADVANCED). Temperature upper bound averaged with other sources to 27°C. Care level resolved to intermediate by averaging across sources.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens — Trachyphyllia Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked lighting (50–100 PAR preferred, 30–150 range), Ca ~425 ppm, Alk 7–9 dKH, Mg ~1350 ppm, flow low-medium, feeding schedule (multiple times/week with mysis/brine/krill/pellets), nitrate/phosphate targets. Confirms sand-bed placement requirement.

additional.name: Boodleshire Aquatics — Coral Profile: Trachyphyllia
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.022–1.025), dKH (7.8–12), Ca (400–480 ppm), Mg (1300–1400 ppm), temp (76–78°F / 24.4–25.6°C), lighting (50–100 PAR), max colony size (20 cm), minimum tank size (20 US gal / 76 L, rounded to 100 L conservatively). Confirms bleaching risk above 150 PAR.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Trachyphyllia (taxonomy and biology)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Merulinidae, Order Scleractinia), monotypic genus confirmation, native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to New Caledonia), depth range (max 40 m), colony size (rarely exceeds 20 cm), habitat (sandy reef slopes and lagoons), synonyms (Wellsophyllia radiata, Antillia geoffroyi, Turbinolia geoffroyi), and IUCN Near Threatened status. Not used for husbandry parameters.
